affected_groups: disadvantaged and Roma students experiencing ongoing segregation
future policymakers relying on monitoring data
content.decision_question: Is it legitimate to continue monitoring segregation indicators indefinitely while withholding structural action, or does the government's own acknowledgment of harm create an obligation to act despite political costs?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can document whether monitoring data has historically preceded policy action in comparable cases and can track worsening indicators over time, but it cannot determine the threshold of harm or acknowledgment that obligates action—that judgment rests on political and ethical values regarding acceptable delay, not on empirical findings.
content.tension: Continuing to monitor segregation and equity outcomes without structural action generates useful data but leaves the Minister's own acknowledged harms unaddressed, creating a credibility gap between stated concern and enacted policy, particularly since existing assessment infrastructure has a documented history of not translating into structural change.
content.title: Monitoring as accountability tool versus monitoring as policy avoidance
value_poles: urgency of acting on acknowledged harm
